title: Supercooling of liquids, as described by the Enskog-Vlasov kinetic equation
abstract: A model combining Enskog's collision integral for dense fluids with a Vlasov-style description of the van der Waals force is applied to supercooling. First, the spinodal temperature $T_{s}$ is calculated, at which a liquid becomes unstable to small perturbations and transitions to solid. In particular, it turns out that isochoric cooling allows one to reach a lower temperature than isobaric cooling. Second, the surface tension of a supercooled liquid-vapor interface is shown to diverge at $T_{s}$. The singularity is caused by an oscillatory region emerging on the liquid side of the interface as $T\rightarrow T_{s}$; it develops because the liquid approaches instability, and the interface starts radiating (so far, evanescent) waves. At $T=T_{s}$, the waves cease to be evanescent and the oscillatory region extends to infinity -- hence, the singularity of the surface tension. Since this effect has a clear physical interpretation, it should occur regardless of the model and approximations under which it was obtained. This and the other results of the paper are illustrated using argon and several other fluids.
summary: This work applies the Enskog-Vlasov kinetic equation to model the supercooling of liquids, predicting that the surface tension diverges at the spinodal temperature ($T_s$). The analysis uses stability criteria derived from the kinetic model to map out the instability region, offering a theoretical understanding of the liquid-to-solid transition.
main_problem: The paper investigates the supercooling of liquids, focusing on determining the spinodal temperature and analyzing the behavior of the liquid-vapor interface as the liquid approaches instability.
main_result: The Enskog-Vlasov model predicts that the surface tension of a supercooled liquid diverges at the spinodal temperature ($T_s$), which is linked to the emergence of oscillatory waves at the interface.
method: The study employs the Enskog-Vlasov (EV) kinetic equation, combining Enskog's collision integral with a Vlasov-style description of the van der Waals force, and uses stability analysis on the resulting equations.
model_or_system: The system is supercooled fluids (e.g., Argon, $	ext{N}_2$, $	ext{H}_2	ext{O}$). The model is the Enskog-Vlasov (EV) kinetic equation, which is calibrated using macroscopic parameters like critical and triple-point values.
key_observables: Spinodal temperature ($T_s$), surface tension ($\gamma$), density profile $n(z)$, and the divergence of $\gamma$ at $T_s$.
important_parameters: Molecular diameter ($D$), van der Waals constant ($a$), Korteweg constant ($K$), and the ratio $\Delta T/T_{tp}$ for different cooling paths (isobaric vs. isochoric).
main_assumptions: The model assumes a nonrotational EV description and relies on series expansions (e.g., up to $L=5$) and phenomenological calibration against empirical data.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the divergence of surface tension ($\gamma$) vs. temperature ($T$) near $T_s$, and stability portraits in the density-temperature plane showing the spinodal boundaries for various fluids.
paper_structure: The paper develops the EV model, calculates thermodynamic properties and the liquid-vapor interface structure, analyzes stability using wave theory to find the spinodal curve, and compares results across multiple fluids.
why_interesting: The rigorous application of a kinetic equation like EV to predict a fundamental thermodynamic singularity (surface tension divergence) provides a theoretical framework for understanding phase instability in dense matter, which is relevant to non-equilibrium statistical mechanics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. The surface tension γ vs. the temperature T, for argon. The supercooling region, between the spinodal and triple points, is shaded. Curve (t) is computed theoretically, using the EV model (36)–(41); curve (e) corresponds to the empirical formula of Ref. [22]. The latter strictly applies to the range between the triple and critical points, so its exten- sion into the supercooling region is shown by a dotted line.

Fig. 1 was computed for argon. One can see that, when the temperature approaches a certain threshold (which will be referred to as spinodal and denoted by Ts), the surface tension γ appears to tend to infinity. Three comments are in order:

FIG. 2. Spatial structure of liquid–vapor interfaces in argon: ρ = mn is the mass density, z is the coordinate. The temperatures are: (1) T = 145 K, (2) T = 117 K, (3) T = 84 K, (4) T = 55 K. For comparison, Tcr ≈151 K, Ttp ≈84 K, and Ts ≈40.6 K.

FIG. 3. Stability portrait of argon in the density-temperature plane. The curves labeled k = 0 and k = k1.5 correspond to spinodal instabilities with respect to infinitely-long and finite- length waves, respectively. Curves (c), (b), and (f)—the last two virtually coincident—show the isoChoric, isoBaric, and interFacial trajectories, respectively. The small empty circle denotes the critical point, the shaded circle denotes the triple point.

FIG. 4. The stability portraits of argon, nitrogen & carbon monoxide, oxygen & fluorine, and water, in terms of the nondimen- sional variables, nnd = D3n and Tnd = kBT/am2D3. The small empty circles show the fluids’ critical points; the curves passing through them correspond to marginal stability with respect to infinitely long waves. The near-vertical curves correspond to instability at finite wavelengths. The shaded circles show the fluids’ triple points.
